title: Structured Output
description: Learn how to reliably get Claude to return JSON and other structured data formats — essential for building real applications.
learningObjectives: Get Claude to reliably output valid JSON
Handle edge cases in structured extraction

sections.title: Why Structured Output Matters
sections.content:
When you're building applications with Claude, you almost always need structured data — not free-form text. You need JSON you can parse, arrays you can iterate over, or objects you can store in a database.

The challenge is that Claude is fundamentally a text generator. Left to its own devices, it'll give you a nicely formatted explanation when you really need parseable JSON. Getting reliable structured output requires deliberate prompting strategies.

There are several approaches, from simple to robust:
1. **Prompting for JSON** — Tell Claude to respond in JSON format
2. **Schema-guided output** — Provide the exact schema you expect
3. **Prefilling** — Start Claude's response with `{` to force JSON mode
4. **Tool use for structure** — Define a tool whose input schema matches your desired output

sections.title: Prompting for JSON
sections.content:
The simplest approach is to explicitly tell Claude to respond in JSON:

```
Extract the following information from this product review
and return it as JSON:

- product_name (string)
- rating (number, 1-5)
- pros (array of strings)
- cons (array of strings)
- would_recommend (boolean)

Review: "The XR-500 headphones are amazing! Great sound
quality and comfortable for long sessions. Battery life
could be better though. Solid 4 out of 5."

Respond with only the JSON object, no other text.
```

Two key tips for reliable JSON output:

1. **"Respond with only the JSON object, no other text"** — This prevents Claude from adding explanations before or after the JSON.

2. **Specify the exact field names and types** — Don't leave Claude guessing what to call things. Be explicit about `string`, `number`, `boolean`, and `array` types.

You can make this even more reliable by providing an example of the exact output format you expect.

playground.description: Try getting Claude to output valid JSON. Experiment with how you specify the fields and types.
playground.systemPrompt: You extract structured data from text. Always respond with only a JSON object, no other text.
playground.userMessage:
Extract product information from this review:

"The XR-500 headphones are amazing! Great sound quality and comfortable for long sessions. Battery life could be better though. Solid 4 out of 5."

Return JSON with: product_name (string), rating (number 1-5), pros (array), cons (array)

sections.title: Schema-Guided Output

playground.description: Try providing a TypeScript schema to get reliable structured output. Experiment with adding or removing fields.
playground.systemPrompt: You extract event information from text. Return a JSON object matching the provided schema exactly. Respond with only the JSON, no other text.

sections.title: Design a Structured Extraction Prompt
sections.content: Let's practice designing a prompt that reliably extracts structured data.

exercise.prompt:
You're building a recipe app that needs to extract structured data from free-text recipe descriptions. Design a prompt (system prompt + user message format) that will extract:

- Recipe name
- Prep time and cook time (in minutes)
- Servings
- Ingredients (name, quantity, unit)
- Steps (ordered list)
- Dietary tags (vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, etc.)

Write out the complete system prompt and show an example of how a user message would look. Include how you'd handle edge cases (missing prep time, ambiguous quantities like 'a pinch of salt').
hints: Include a JSON schema or TypeScript type in your system prompt
Show Claude an example of the expected output format
Specify default values for missing information
Think about how to handle non-standard measurements
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) a system prompt with clear role definition, (2) a JSON schema or type definition for the output, (3) explicit handling of edge cases (missing values, ambiguous quantities), (4) an example of the expected output. Strong responses will use a schema that properly types all fields (numbers for times, arrays for ingredients/steps) and handle edge cases gracefully (null for missing values, standardized units).

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Think about the trade-offs between different structured output approaches.

exercise.prompt: What are the advantages of using tool use (function calling) for structured output compared to prompting for JSON directly? When would you choose one approach over the other?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should explain that tool use provides: (1) schema validation — the API enforces the schema, (2) more reliable structure — Claude is trained to follow tool schemas precisely, (3) type safety — you define the schema with proper types. Prompting for JSON is simpler and works well for straightforward extractions. Tool use is better when you need guaranteed schema compliance, are building production systems, or need complex nested structures. Should demonstrate understanding of both approaches.

sections.title: Key Takeaway
sections.content: Reliable structured output requires explicit instructions: define your schema, specify field types, handle edge cases, and tell Claude to respond with only the structured data. For maximum reliability, use the prefilling trick or tool use to enforce JSON output.
